title: Full Curation Exercise
introduction:
This is your final challenge: a complete genome curation from start to finish. You'll return to the finch genome (which you explored in lesson 3) and apply all the skills you've learned.

Your task:
1. Examine the assembly and identify misassemblies
2. Use automated tools (auto-cut and auto-sort) as a starting point
3. Perform manual refinements where needed
4. Produce a high-quality chromosome-level assembly

Work through the steps at your own pace, then use the final checklist to judge your result. Good luck!

steps.title: Survey the Finch Assembly
steps.instruction:
Start by examining the finch genome in its current state. Take your time to:

• Identify major chromosome blocks
• Look for obvious misassemblies (inversions, translocations)
• Count how many chromosomes you expect to find
• Note regions that will need manual correction

Remember: finches typically have around 40 chromosomes, including small microchromosomes. The finch assembly here has 134 contigs, including 32 chromosome-scale scaffolds.
steps.hint: Use zoom and the minimap to scan the entire genome systematically. Make mental notes of problem areas.

steps.title: Detailed Examination
steps.instruction:
Zoom into suspicious regions and confirm your initial observations:

• Do you see butterfly patterns indicating inversions?
• Are there off-diagonal blocks suggesting translocations?
• Are chromosome boundaries clean, or are contigs misplaced?

Toggle the contig grid on to see boundaries more clearly. Open the sidebar to see contig lengths and names.
steps.hint: Focus on the largest chromosomes first. Small microchromosomes can be handled later.

steps.title: Apply Auto-Cut
steps.instruction:
OpenPretext provides automated tools to help with curation. The auto-cut tool analyzes the contact map and automatically cuts contigs at likely misassembly points.

Switch to Edit mode, then run auto-cut: press Option+C (Alt+C on Windows), or open the Command Palette (Cmd/Ctrl+K) and choose 'Auto cut'. Accept the default sensitivity when prompted. Review the cuts it makes. Not all will be correct, but it's a good starting point.

Remember: you can undo any operation, so don't hesitate to try it.
steps.hint: Auto-cut uses signal analysis to find break points. It's not perfect but handles obvious cases well.

steps.title: Apply Auto-Sort
steps.instruction:
After cutting, reorder contigs to maximize diagonal signal. The auto-sort tool uses Union-Find greedy chaining on Hi-C link scores to find a good ordering.

In Edit mode, run auto-sort: press Option+S (Alt+S on Windows), or open the Command Palette (Cmd/Ctrl+K) and choose 'Auto sort'. Accept the default link threshold when prompted. This can take a few seconds for large genomes.

Watch the contact map transform as contigs are rearranged. Chromosome blocks should become more distinct and the diagonal should strengthen.
steps.hint: Auto-sort works best after you've used auto-cut to break up large misassemblies. The two tools complement each other.

steps.title: Review Automated Results
steps.instruction:
Examine the results of auto-cut and auto-sort:

• Are chromosome blocks now cleaner and more distinct?
• Do you still see misassembly patterns?
• Are there regions that need manual intervention?

The automated tools handle ~80-90% of the work, but expert curation requires human judgment for the remaining cases.
steps.hint: Zoom into each chromosome block systematically. Look for remaining inversions or translocations that the automated tools missed.

steps.title: Manual Refinement
steps.instruction:
Now apply your manual curation skills to fix remaining issues:

• Cut any contigs that still contain inversions
• Join adjacent contigs with strong contact signal
• Invert contigs showing anti-diagonal patterns
• Reorder contigs to maximize diagonal signal

This is where your expertise matters. Take your time and make thoughtful edits. Use undo freely to experiment.
steps.hint: Focus on high-impact changes. Fixing inversions in large contigs will improve the assembly more than tweaking small pieces.

steps.title: Final Quality Check
steps.instruction:
Before completing this lesson, do a final quality check:

• Zoom out and view the entire genome
• Each chromosome should be a clean, bright square block
• The diagonal should be strong and continuous
• Off-diagonal signal should be minimal except at centromeres
• Open the stats panel to see assembly metrics (N50, L50, etc.)
• Open the 3D Analysis Results panel and find the 'Join support' row. It reads 'N weak' or 'all supported'. After the auto-sort and auto-join you just did, any remaining weak junctions are worth a second look.
• Toggle tracks (X) to show the red 'Weak joins' markers, then navigate to each flagged junction and re-examine it. A real chromosome join has contact continuing across the boundary; a weak one shows a dark off-diagonal square and may need another cut or reorder.

When you're satisfied that the assembly meets these criteria, complete the lesson.
steps.hint: A good chromosome-level assembly has: clear boundaries, strong diagonal signal, minimal artifacts, high N50/L50 values, and 'all supported' (or only intentional chromosome boundaries) on the Join support row. Use the red Weak joins markers to jump straight to any suspect junction.
